Hierarchical linear model on fish population trends

Model we are attempting to build:

\[N = f(t, \alpha_i) + g(\lambda, \beta_i) + \epsilon\]

Where:

  • \(N\) : population size estimate
  • \(t\) : time step
  • \(i\) : population
  • \(f(t, \alpha_i)\) : function estimating the temporal trend in population size
    • \(\alpha\): each population’s estimated temporal slope
  • \(g(\lambda, \beta_i)\) : function estimating latent variation in population size
    • \(\lambda\) : latent variable (to capture covariation between populations)
    • \(\beta\) : estimated parameter describing each popultion’s latent variation
  • \(\epsilon\): residual variation

Because the model also estimates latent variation, we are accounting for the covariation between populations when estimating \(N\).

This model then contains key information to calculate and extend an index like the Living Planet Index, to show the average change in population size from a baseline value of 1 in the start year.

To calculate the LPI, the estimated global change in population size \(N\) can be plugged into the calculation to get the overall growth rate:

\[dt = log_{10} (\frac{N_t}{N_{t-1}}) \]

Which can then be used to calculate the LPI as:

\[ I_t = I_{t-1}*10^{\bar{dt}} \]

The confidence intervals for the index can be obtained directly from the residual error in the model estimating \(N\), which propagates through the calculation of the log-ratio growth rate into the transformation into the index of change \(I_t\).

Setting up

#### import & set up data ####

# from https://github.com/eric-pedersen/groundfish-data-analysis
# note: these are mean biomasses over the whole sampled area with an SE
# not actually raw abundances. But for this purpose, should be ok
load("~/Documents/GitHub/groundfish-data-analysis/data/year_geom_means.Rdata")

time = rownames(Year_Geom_Means_all) %>% as.numeric()

# get number of populations & length of time series
npops <- ncol(Year_Geom_Means_all)
tsl <- nrow(Year_Geom_Means_all)

Building the model with HMSC

# format data
XData <- as.matrix(time)
YData <- as.matrix(Year_Geom_Means_all)
randomEff <- as.factor(1:tsl) # assign a location to pairings of pops

# prepare data
formDat <- as.HMSCdata(X = XData, Y = YData, Random = randomEff)

# run model
m <- hmsc(formDat,
                family = "gaussian",
                niter = 15000,
                nburn = 5000,
                thin = 5,
                verbose = FALSE)
# save model
saveRDS(m, here("models/hmsc.RDS"))

Model performance

# extract the parameters and format them into an `mcmc` object.
mcmcMeansParamX <- as.mcmc(m, parameters = "meansParamX")

# check for parameter convergence (visually)
traceplot(mcmcMeansParamX)

Extract model coefficients

# extract the parameters and format them into an `mcmc` object.
coef(m)

Explanatory power

# model explanatory power

# (R2 <- Rsquared(m, averageSp = FALSE))
(R2comm <- Rsquared(m, averageSp = TRUE))
## [1] 0.6039956

Population trends (\(f(t, \alpha_i)\))

Covariance between populations

# extract all estimated species-to-species associations matrix
assoMat <- corRandomEff(m)

# Average
siteMean <- apply(assoMat[, , , 1], 1:2, mean)

# Plot as heatmap
corrplot::corrplot(siteMean, type = "lower",
                   method = "color", 
                   tl.cex = .4, tl.col = "black")

Global population trend

# Predict and plot model over time 

# prepare and format data
formPredDat = as.HMSCdata(X = XData, Random = randomEff)
## [1] "'Random' was included into a data.frame"
## [1] "column names were added to 'X'"
## [1] "row names were added to 'X'"
# predict values
pred <- predict(m,
                newdata = formPredDat,
                type = "response")
# Get mean trend across populations

# take the mean N per year
pred_mean = apply(pred, 1, mean)

Rates of population change

# calculate log-ratio population change
calc_dt <- function(N){
  dt = c(1) # initial value
  for(i in 2:length(N)){
    dt[i] = log10(N[i]/N[i-1])
  }
  return(dt)
}

dt = apply(pred, 2, calc_dt)

dt_bar = apply(dt, 1, mean, na.rm = TRUE)
dt_ci = apply(dt, 1, quantile, probs = c(0.025, 0.975), na.rm = TRUE)
dt_var = apply(dt, 1, var, na.rm = TRUE)

Calculate an index of average change (à la LPI)

calc_lpi <- function(rate){
  lpi = 1
  for(t in 2:tsl){
    lpi[t] = lpi[t-1]*10^rate[t]
  }
  return(lpi)
}

lpi = calc_lpi(dt_bar)
lpi_pops = apply(dt, 2, calc_lpi)

# plot lpi and all population lpis
matplot(lpi_pops, x = time, 
        type = "l", lwd = 0.2, lty = 1, 
        ylim = c(0, 5))
lines(x = time, y = lpi, lwd = 3)

Show uncertainty in the mean
